name: Sástago and Bujaraloz Salt Flats Loop
name_original: Saladas de Sástago y Bujaraloz — gravel circular por Los Monegros
description: This difficult 33.9-mile gravel cycling route in Los Monegros explores unique saline lagoons and arid landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Caspe traverse a diverse landscape shaped by the River Ebro and the Mequinenza Reservoir. The region features varied terrain, including river valleys, canyons, and areas with unique saline lagoons and arid environments. Geological formations from the Iberian and Catalonian Coastal Ranges contribute to its rugged character, offering a mix of flat sections and significant elevation changes. Best touring cycling routes around Caspe The most popular touring cycling route is Sástago and Bujaraloz Salt Flats Loop, a 33.9 miles (54.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 34 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores unique saline landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Way of St. James: Ebro Meanders near Escatrón, a moderate 15.6 miles (25.2 km) path. It follows the scenic meanders of the River Ebro. Local touring cyclists also love the Castillo y Pueblo Antiguo de Mequinenza, Montnegre and Sierra de Mequinenza, a 15.3 miles (24.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain including historical sites and mountain views, often completed in about 1 hour 54 minutes. Touring cycling around Caspe is defined by river valleys, the expansive Mequinenza Reservoir, and rugged rock formations. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy rides to challenging routes with significant elevation gains. The routes in Caspe are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Caspe's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Caspe, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: There are over 45 touring cycling routes around Caspe. These include 2 easy routes, 17 moderate routes, and 26 difficult routes, offering options for various fitness levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling around Caspe?
A: Cycling around Caspe offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll encounter scenic meanders of the &lt;strong&gt;River Ebro&lt;/strong&gt;, the expansive &lt;strong&gt;Mequinenza Reservoir&lt;/strong&gt; (also known as the Mar de Aragón), unique saline lagoons, arid environments, and rugged terrain with rock formations and mountain views. Routes often traverse river valleys and canyons, showcasing the geological character shaped by the Iberian and Catalonian Coastal Ranges.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in the Caspe area?
A: Yes, Caspe offers some family-friendly touring cycling options. For an easier ride suitable for families, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14888237" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Ebro – Sea of Aragon loop from Valdemoro&lt;/a&gt;. This route is rated easy and provides pleasant views without significant elevation challenges.

Q: What are some notable historical sites or attractions I can visit along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes incorporate cultural and historical points of interest. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4644675" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Village of Mequinenza&lt;/a&gt;, or pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6444930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Church Tower of Fayón&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features the &lt;strong&gt;Castillo del Compromiso&lt;/strong&gt; in Caspe itself, which is a significant historical attraction. For a route that combines history with varied terrain, try the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993055684" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo y Pueblo Antiguo de Mequinenza, Montnegre and Sierra de Mequinenza&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available in Caspe?
A: Yes, there are several circular touring cycling routes. A challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993704276" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sástago and Bujaraloz Salt Flats Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which explores unique saline landscapes. Another circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993711285" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillete de Fayón y Mausoleo de Fabara — circular desde Nonaspe&lt;/a&gt;, offering a scenic loop from Nonaspe.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Caspe?
A: The Caspe region experiences a Mediterranean climate, making spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) ideal for touring cycling. During these seasons, temperatures are generally mild and pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. This allows for comfortable exploration of the diverse terrain.

Q: Where can I find routes that follow the River Ebro?
A: The River Ebro is a prominent feature in the Caspe area, and several routes follow its scenic course. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993699503" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Way of St. James: Ebro Meanders near Escatrón&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate ride along the river. The Ebro Nature Trail (GR 99), Spain's longest river, also passes through the region, offering extensive riverside cycling opportunities.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Mequinenza Reservoir?
A: Absolutely. The Mequinenza Reservoir, also known as the Mar de Aragón, is a major highlight. You can find stunning views along routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23753211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Compromise – View of Lake and Mountains loop from Caspe&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, specific viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4601307" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Ebro River at Mequinenza Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5254812" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Ermita de la Magdalena from the Mequinenza Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; are accessible and offer panoramic vistas.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Caspe?
A: The touring cycling routes in Caspe are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from river valleys to rugged rock formations, and the opportunities to explore historical sites and natural beauty. Over 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to discover the area's diverse offerings.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through or near the town of Sástago?
A: Yes, the town of Sástago is featured in some of the touring cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143619" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre del Tambor of Sástago – Sástago loop from Alborge&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that includes this area. You can also visit &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5631540" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sástago&lt;/a&gt; as a highlight within the region.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the more challenging routes?
A: The more challenging routes around Caspe often feature significant elevation gains and varied surfaces. You can expect a mix of paved roads and unpaved sections, including gravel paths. The geological structure of the area, with its rugged character, means these routes can include climbs through mountain views and canyons, requiring good to very good fitness.

Q: Is there public transport available to access cycling routes around Caspe?
A: While Caspe is accessible by public transport, direct access to the start points of all cycling routes may vary. It's advisable to check local bus or train schedules to Caspe town center and then plan your cycling route from there, or consider bringing your bike on regional transport if permitted. Many routes are designed to start from towns or villages within the region.
tips: Located in the Los Monegros region, the town of Bujarlaoz has numerous works that we can visit; the Church of Santiago el Mayor restored in the 16th century, the Hermitage of Mercy (better known today as the Virgen de las Nieves), the Hermitage of San Antón or the Torres Solanot Palace. We can also find different rafts with which the town was supplied with water.

At this point, we can approach the Lagoon along the path and we will have beautiful views of the Laguna de la Playa (the largest and most representative of the salt lakes that make up the complex) that we find in the Saladas de Sástago, a unique saline landscape in Europe that fills up completely only in times of abundant rain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Saladas de Sastago y Bujaraloz gravel circular route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features unpaved roads and tracks, typical of gravel cycling in the arid, steppe-like environment of Los Monegros. You'll encounter surfaces that can be challenging, with majestic rock formations and unique geological features like gypsum, marl, and limestone, especially around the distinctive saline wetlands.

faqs.question: Is this gravel route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult for touring bicycles. While not overly steep, the unpaved surfaces, exposure to elements like strong winds (cierzo or bochorno), and the overall length of nearly 55 km make it more suitable for experienced gravel cyclists rather than beginners.

faqs.question: What unique natural features or wildlife can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The main attraction is the complex of saline lagoons, particularly the View of the Saladas de Sástago. This area is a significant Ramsar wetland site, home to specialized flora and fauna adapted to saline and extreme climatic conditions. It's a haven for naturalists and ornithologists, offering a glimpse into a unique ecosystem often compared to the Atacama Desert.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Monegros region has a continental climate with hot summers and dry winters. The best times to cycle are typically spring and autumn to avoid the intense heat of summer and the potential for colder, windier conditions in winter. Be prepared for strong winds (cierzo or bochorno) regardless of the season.

faqs.question: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several points of interest. You can see the Church of Santiago el Mayor of Bujaraloz, enjoy the View of the Saladas de Sástago, and explore the Hidden Water Heritage of the Steppe, which offers insight into the area's historical water management.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead for this circular route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details for the start of this exact circular route are not provided, routes in the Bujaraloz area typically involve parking in or near the town before heading onto the unpaved tracks. It's advisable to check local parking options in Bujaraloz before your trip.

faqs.question: Is the Saladas de Sastago y Bujaraloz gravel circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in many natural areas, this route traverses a significant Ramsar wetland site with unique and sensitive flora and fauna. To protect the delicate ecosystem and wildlife, it's highly recommended to keep dogs on a leash at all times, especially near the lagoons. Always practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee to cycle in Los Monegros or the Saladas de Sástago area?
faqs.answer: There are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the general Los Monegros region or the Saladas de Sástago area for cycling. However, as it is a protected Ramsar wetland site, visitors are expected to respect the natural environment, stay on marked paths, and avoid disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route briefly intersect with the Camí Català per Lleida (covering about 11% of the route) and Calle Arrabal B (a very small portion).

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or water sources along the route?
faqs.answer: The route traverses a remote and arid landscape. Amenities like cafes or dedicated water sources are extremely limited, if available at all, directly on the trail. It is crucial to carry sufficient water, snacks, and any necessary supplies for the entire duration of your ride. Bujaraloz would be the most likely place to find services before or after your trip.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a gravel cycling trip on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty, length, and remote nature, you should bring plenty of water, high-energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike (including spare tubes), navigation tools (like a GPS device or the komoot app), sun protection (sunscreen, hat, sunglasses), and appropriate clothing for varying weather conditions, including potential strong winds. A first-aid kit is also recommended.
